description:
<description>
    You are working as a web-developer who has just been handed yesterday’s Apache access log so you can see where visitors are going and how your application is behaving.  
    The (already existing) log file is located at:

      /home/user/project/logs/access.log

    1. Create a new directory called /home/user/project/reports  
       (make sure it is created even if it does not yet exist).

    2. From the access.log file, generate TWO frequency reports using classic
       Unix text-processing tools (e.g. cut, awk, sort, uniq, etc.).

       a. HTTP status-code frequency report  
          • Output file name: /home/user/project/reports/status_counts.tsv  
          • Output format (tab-separated, **exactly one TAB between columns**):  
              Header line:  
                count<TAB>status  
              One data row per distinct status code.  
          • Sorting rule for the data rows:  
              – first by count in descending numerical order,  
              – when counts are equal, by status code in ascending
                numerical order.  

       b. Request-endpoint frequency report  
          • Output file name: /home/user/project/reports/endpoint_counts.tsv  
          • Output format (tab-separated, **exactly one TAB between columns**):  
              Header line:  
                count<TAB>endpoint  
              One data row per distinct HTTP request target (the path part that
              appears right after the HTTP method).  
          • Sorting rule for the data rows:  
              – first by count in descending numerical order,  
              – when counts are equal, by endpoint in ascending
                ASCII order.

    3. Both .tsv files MUST end with a single trailing newline, must not contain
       any additional blank lines and must follow the header-first, data-after
       convention exactly as described above so that automated tests can parse
       them without ambiguity.

    4. When everything is done, show a preview in the terminal by printing the
       two output files to STDOUT (e.g. with cat) so a human reviewer can see
       the result.

    Do not hard-code the numbers: derive them from the log file so the solution
    continues to work if the log contents change.  All work must stay inside
    /home/user; you do not need root privileges.
  </description>
truth:

  <!-- Expected report contents for grading -->

  <expected_file path="/home/user/project/reports/status_counts.tsv"><![CDATA[
